title: Rules of the Pool
learningGoal: The learner will decode narrative text to successfully identify internal semantic stratifications between permissive 'can', advisory 'should', and obligatory 'must' within a regulatory context.
instructions: Read the story about Amit visiting a public swimming pool. Pay attention to the rules! Notice the difference in power between words like 'Can' (Allowed), 'Should' (a good idea), and 'Must' (a strong rule). Answer the 8 questions below.

contentItems.text: Watch the verbs! They never change after Can, Should, or Must!

readingText: Amit wants to swim. He goes to the city pool. At the entrance, he sees a big sign with the rules. The sign says: 'You can swim from 9 AM to 8 PM.' Amit is happy. He walks inside. The lifeguard looks at Amit. 'It is very hot today,' the lifeguard says. 'You should wear a hat and you should drink water. To stay safe, you must not run near the water. The floor is wet. If you run, you will fall.' Amit nods his head. He promises to walk slowly. Then, the lifeguard points at the deep area of the pool. 'You can swim there if you are a good swimmer, but children must stay in the shallow water. And everyone must shower before they enter the pool.' Amit takes a shower. He can swim very fast, but he remembers the rules. He walks slowly to the deep water and has a great day.
textInTargetLanguage: Amit wants to swim. He goes to the city pool. At the entrance, he sees a big sign with the rules. The sign says: 'You can swim from 9 AM to 8 PM.' Amit is happy. He walks inside. The lifeguard looks at Amit. 'It is very hot today,' the lifeguard says. 'You should wear a hat and you should drink water. To stay safe, you must not run near the water. The floor is wet. If you run, you will fall.' Amit nods his head. He promises to walk slowly. Then, the lifeguard points at the deep area of the pool. 'You can swim there if you are a good swimmer, but children must stay in the shallow water. And everyone must shower before they enter the pool.' Amit takes a shower. He can swim very fast, but he remembers the rules. He walks slowly to the deep water and has a great day.
baseLanguageSupportText: अमित तैरना चाहता है। वह शहर के पूल में जाता है। प्रवेश द्वार पर, वह नियमों के साथ एक बड़ा बोर्ड देखता है। बोर्ड पर लिखा है: 'आप सुबह 9 बजे से रात 8 बजे तक तैर सकते हैं।' अमित खुश है। वह अंदर जाता है। लाइफगार्ड अमित को देखता है। 'आज बहुत गर्मी है,' लाइफगार्ड कहता है। 'आपको टोपी पहननी चाहिए और आपको पानी पीना चाहिए। सुरक्षित रहने के लिए, आपको पानी के पास नहीं दौड़ना चाहिए (सख्त मनाही है)। फर्श गीला है। यदि आप दौड़ेंगे, तो आप गिर जाएंगे।' अमित सिर हिलाता है। वह धीरे चलने का वादा करता है। फिर, लाइफगार्ड पूल के गहरे क्षेत्र की ओर इशारा करता है। 'अगर आप अच्छे तैराक हैं तो आप वहाँ तैर सकते हैं, लेकिन बच्चों को उथले पानी में ही रहना होगा (अनिवार्य है)। और पूल में प्रवेश करने से पहले सभी को नहाना चाहिए (अनिवार्य)।' अमित नहाता है। वह बहुत तेज़ तैर सकता है, लेकिन उसे नियम याद हैं। वह धीरे-धीरे गहरे पानी की ओर जाता है और एक शानदार दिन बिताता है।

exampleSentence.baseTranslation: आप सुबह 9 बजे से तैर सकते हैं।

exampleSentence.targetText: You must not run near the water.
exampleSentence.transliteration: यू मस्ट नॉट रन नियर द वाटर।
exampleSentence.baseTranslation: आपको पानी के पास (बिल्कुल) नहीं दौड़ना चाहिए।

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: You can swim from 9 AM to 8 PM.
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: यू कैन स्विम फ्रॉम 9 एएम टू 8 पीएम।
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: आप सुबह 9 बजे से रात 8 बजे तक तैर सकते हैं। (Permitted)

sentenceSupportPairs.targetText: Children must stay in the shallow water.
sentenceSupportPairs.transliteration: चिल्ड्रन मस्ट स्टे इन द शैलो वाटर।
sentenceSupportPairs.baseTranslation: बच्चों को उथले पानी में ही (अनिवार्य) रहना होगा।

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: What does the sign mean when it says 'You can swim from 9 AM'?
The lifeguard says 'You should wear a hat.' This means:
options: It is a good idea, but not a strong rule.
It is the law of the pool.
You are not allowed to wear a hat.

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: What is the absolute strongest rule the lifeguard says you MUST NOT do?

options: Because the floor is wet and you will fall

comprehensionQuestions.questionText: Look closely at the grammar of this sentence from the story: 'Children must stay in the shallow water.' Which word is completely MISSING, proving the grammar rule?
What must EVERYONE do before they enter the pool?
Does the story say Amit 'can to swim very fast' or 'can swim very fast'?
If Amit broke the rule and started running, the lifeguard would likely say: 'You _____ stop running now!'

correctAnswers.q2: It is a good idea, but not a strong rule.
correctAnswers.q4: Because the floor is wet and you will fall

expectedSummaryPoints: Learners differentiate 'Must' as absolute obligation vs 'Should' as mere advice.
Learners verify that 'to' never interpolates between the modal and the infinitive verb (children must stay, not must to stay).

feedbackRules.onPass: Magnificent! You perfectly decoded the rules of the pool and you understand the huge power difference between 'Should' and 'Must'!
feedbackRules.onFail: Keep trying! Remember: Can = Permission. Should = Good idea/Advice. Must = Absolute Rule. Also, look at the grammar: 'MUST STAY'. It is NEVER 'Must to stay'!
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good effort! Watch out for Question 5. The word 'to' is strictly forbidden after Modals. The text says 'Must stay', proving the rule!

audioAssets.audioTranscript: Amit wants to swim... (full transcript provided in text)

hintText: Read the lifeguard's speech carefully. Why does he use 'should' for drinking water, but 'must not' for running?
commonMistakes: Thinking 'Should' is a strong rule, when it is just polite advice.
